preamble: The shift from the alveolar stop [d] to the high-front vowel [i] (or the palatal glide [j]) is a form of vocalization. It typically happens in two contexts: as a stage of extreme intervocalic lenition, or when palatalized by a neighboring front vowel.

examples.note: Grammatical lenition (séimhiú) of 'slender d' results in a palatal glide/vowel.
The palatalized dental stop shifted entirely to a palatal glide/vowel.

sources: Thurneysen: A Grammar of Old Irish
